# The Impact of Technology on Mental Health: Tips for a Healthy Digital Life

![The Impact of Technology on Mental Health: Tips for a Healthy Digital Life](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20905%20400'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![The Impact of Technology on Mental Health: Tips for a Healthy Digital Life](https://texaspsychiatrygroup.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/The-Impact-of-Technology-on-Mental-Health-Tips-for-a-Healthy-Digital-Life.jpg)

Technology has become a huge part of our daily lives – from checking our phones first thing in the morning to using apps for work, socializing, and entertainment. While tech has made things easier, it also comes with its fair share of challenges, especially when it comes to our mental health. The constant notifications, the pressure to be “available” all the time, and the never-ending scrolling can have a real impact on how we feel. In this article, we’ll dive into how technology affects mental health, focusing on stress, anxiety, and other common issues, while offering some practical tips to help keep your digital life balanced and healthy.

## Technology: A Blessing and a Curse

There’s no doubt about it – technology has made life more convenient. We can work from anywhere, stay in touch with friends and family no matter where they are, and have information at our fingertips. But the more we rely on it, the more we’re seeing how it can impact our well-being. Let’s break it down.

### 1. Stress from Constant Connectivity

It’s easy to feel overwhelmed when you’re constantly plugged in. Whether it’s emails, social media updates, text messages, or work-related alerts, we’re always getting hit with new information. While technology was meant to make our lives easier, it often ends up creating more pressure. The constant stream of notifications can raise our stress levels and leave us feeling like we can never catch a break. High levels of screen time, especially throughout the day, can increase cortisol (the stress hormone) in our bodies, making it harder to relax.

### 2. Social Media and Anxiety

Social media plays a significant role in contributing to tech-related anxiety. While it’s great to stay connected with friends and family, the pressure to constantly post, share, and look “perfect” can lead to stress and self-doubt. We scroll through curated images of others living their best lives and compare ourselves. This leads to feelings of inadequacy and, for many, social anxiety. It’s easy to get trapped in a cycle of checking how many likes or comments you get, which only adds to the pressure.

### 3. How Screens Affect Our Sleep

Another big issue is the impact of technology on sleep. If you’ve ever found yourself scrolling through your phone late at night, you’re not alone. But the blue light from screens can actually mess with our sleep. It tricks our brains into thinking it’s still daytime, making it harder to fall asleep. Lack of sleep is closely tied to **mental health problems** like [anxiety](https://texaspsychiatrygroup.com/blog/managing-anxiety-attacks-understanding-triggers-frequency-and-treatment-options-in-texas/) and [depression](https://texaspsychiatrygroup.com/blog/recognizing-the-signs-of-depression/), creating a cycle where poor sleep leads to worsened mental health, and worsened mental health makes it even harder to sleep.

### 4. The Isolation Paradox

Ironically, while technology connects us to others, it can also make us feel more isolated. Spending too much time online, whether on social media or playing games, can leave you feeling lonely. When we spend more time on our screens, we often neglect face-to-face interactions that are vital for emotional well-being. Over time, this can contribute to feelings of loneliness and even depression.

## The Common Mental Health Issues Linked to Technology

As we’ve seen, excessive screen time and social media use can lead to a variety of mental health problems, including:

**– Tech-related Stress:** The feeling that you’re always “on” and available for work or social updates can lead to burnout and anxiety.

**– Social Media Anxiety:** Constant comparison to others can affect self-esteem and cause feelings of anxiety.

**– FOMO (Fear of Missing Out):** Watching others live what seems like a better life can create feelings of insecurity and stress.

**– Sleep Disruption:** The habit of checking screens late into the night leads to poor sleep, which worsens symptoms of depression and anxiety.

**– Digital Addiction:** Spending too much time online can lead to a dependency on digital devices, which negatively impacts real-world relationships and emotional health.

## Tips for a Healthy Digital Life

If you’re feeling overwhelmed by the digital world, don’t worry – you’re not alone, and there are steps you can take to regain control. Here are some practical tips to help you create a healthier relationship with technology:

### 1. Set Limits on Screen Time

Spending all day on your phone or computer can be draining, both mentally and physically. Try setting daily limits on screen time. Most smartphones have built-in features that allow you to track and limit your usage of certain apps. Try setting a timer for social media apps or turning off notifications to reduce the pressure of being constantly available.

### 2. Create Tech-Free Zones

Designate certain areas of your home as “tech-free zones.” For example, make your bedroom a no-phone zone, and try leaving your devices in another room when you’re eating meals or spending time with loved ones. This will give you a chance to unplug and focus on real-world connections without distractions.

### 3. Be Mindful of Your Social Media Use

Social media can be a great way to stay connected, but it can also be a source of stress if not used mindfully. Set specific times during the day to check your social accounts and unfollow or mute any accounts that trigger negative emotions. Follow accounts that inspire and uplift you, and take breaks when you feel overwhelmed.

### 4. Take Breaks from Technology

It’s important to take regular breaks from screens throughout the day. This doesn’t just mean stopping to check your phone – it’s about stepping away from your devices and giving your brain a chance to rest. Take a walk, do some deep breathing, or engage in a hobby that doesn’t involve technology. These breaks help reduce stress and boost mental clarity.

### 5. Engage in Offline Hobbies

Reconnect with hobbies or activities that don’t involve screens. Whether it’s reading, cooking, gardening, or going for a run, offline activities help you recharge and keep your mind healthy. They also provide a sense of accomplishment and joy that you may not always get from online interactions.

### 6. Do a Digital Detox

A digital detox is a planned break from all technology. Whether it’s for an hour, a day, or even a weekend, disconnecting from your devices can have a major positive impact on your mental health. Use this time to reconnect with yourself and others without the pressure of digital distractions.

### 7. Seek Professional Help if Needed

If you find that your digital habits are negatively impacting your mental health, don’t hesitate to **seek professional help**. A therapist or counselor can help you navigate anxiety, stress, or digital addiction, and give you the tools to develop a healthier relationship with technology.

#### Healthy Habits for a Digital World: Protecting Your Mental Health

Technology has made life easier in many ways, but it’s important to recognize how it can affect our mental health. The constant stress, anxiety, and distractions brought on by our devices can be overwhelming. However, by taking small steps like limiting screen time, practicing mindful social media use, and setting boundaries for tech use, you can create a healthier digital life. Remember, your mental health should always come first – so don’t be afraid to unplug when you need to. A balanced relationship with technology can help you live a happier, healthier life.

#### Need Extra Support?

If you’re struggling to find balance in your digital life or feel overwhelmed by tech-related stress, **[Texas Psychiatry Group](https://texaspsychiatrygroup.com/)** is here to help. Our experienced professionals provide personalized care to support your mental well-being in today’s fast-paced digital world. Contact us today to take the first step toward a healthier, more balanced you.

SUMMARY:
Implementation Strategies for Digital Mental Health Interventions in Health Care Settings - PMC  Implementation Strategies for Digital Mental Health Interventions in Health Care Settings Specific to DMHI implementation, there has been great progress in identifying facilitators and barriers (see Table 1 and associated references in Supplement 1), establishing frameworks to predict and evaluate the success of patient-facing health care technologies (e.g., the Non-adoption, Abandonment, Scale-up, Spread, and Sustainability framework; Greenhalgh et al., 2017), and defining implementation outcomes (Hermes, Lyon, Schueller, & Glass, 2019). Common barriers and facilitators for implementing digital mental health interventions in health care settings that have been documented in published reviews, and proposed implementation strategies.

Abstract
--------

U.S. health care systems are tasked with alleviating the burden of mental health, but are frequently under-prepared and lack workforce and resource capacity to deliver services to all in need. Digital mental health interventions (DMHIs) can increase access to evidence-based mental health care. However, DMHIs commonly do not fit into the day-to-day activities of the people who engage with them, resulting in a research-to-practice gap for DMHI implementation. For health care settings, differences between digital and traditional mental health services make alignment and integration challenging. Specialized attention is needed to improve the implementation of DMHIs in health care settings so that these services yield high uptake, engagement, and sustainment. The purpose of this paper is to enhance efforts to integrate DMHIs in health care settings by proposing implementation strategies, selected and operationalized based on the discrete strategies established in the Expert Recommendations for Implementing Change project, that align to DMHI-specific barriers in these settings. Guidance is offered in how these strategies can be applied to DMHI implementation across four phases commonly distinguished in implementation science using the Exploration, Preparation, Implementation, Sustainment Framework. Next steps to advance research in this area and improve the research-to-practice gap for implementing DMHIs are recommended. Applying implementation strategies to DMHI implementation will enable psychologists to systematically evaluate this process, which can yield an enhanced understanding of the factors that facilitate implementation success and improve the translation of DMHIs from controlled trials to real-world settings.

**Keywords:** Digital mental health, health care, implementation science, implementation strategies

Introduction
------------

In the United States (U.S.), the 12-month prevalence of mental health problems is nearly 20% in adults and 16.5% in youth ([Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration, 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R53); [Whitney & Peterson, 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R60)). Yet fewer than half of those with mental health needs receive treatment, with significant variation in unmet treatment need by geographic region ([Whitney & Peterson, 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R60)). Health care systems are tasked with addressing these problems, but frequently are under-prepared and lack workforce and resource capacity to deliver services to all in need.

Digital mental health interventions (DMHIs) offer an exciting solution to increase access to mental health services, by supporting existing services (e.g., augmenting traditional services) or providing new services to those in need. DMHIs use online and/or mobile formats to deliver psychological strategies and interventions. DMHIs can range from self-guided tools to facilitate a skill or behavioral strategy (e.g., track activity or mood, self-monitor eating behaviors, increase relaxation/practice meditation), to more complex and comprehensive psychological interventions [e.g., cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) for depression]. Human support (“coaching”) may or may not accompany the intervention (referred to as guided versus unguided interventions, respectively). Innovations are advancing DMHIs, such as by using artificial conversational agents (“chatbots”) to deliver support, delivering virtual/augmented reality interventions or digital interventions for families and groups, and integrating passively-collected sensor data.

Technology can enhance or enable the delivery of mental health services at various points of when care can be provided: while individuals are waiting to receive in-person psychological services, in place of in-person services, as a treatment adjunct while receiving services, and/or following treatment to prevent relapse. There are merits to using different DMHIs for different intervention purposes. Self-guided tools that focus on a specific component of treatment (e.g., self-monitor, track symptoms, practice a particular skill) can be useful adjuncts to treatment by extending content from treatment to day-to-day practice and making it easier for individuals to engage with that component between sessions. By contrast, guided or unguided DMHIs could be an alternative to in-person psychological services, in which treatment is delivered via technology. Meta-analyses of internet-delivered cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) have shown equivalent overall effects with face-to-face treatment for psychiatric and somatic disorders ([Andersson, Cuijpers, Carlbring, Riper, & Hedman, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R4); [Carlbring, Andersson, Cuijpers, Riper, & Hedman-Lagerlof, 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R10)). Further, meta-analyses of mobile interventions for depression and anxiety show moderate average effect sizes of 0.38 and 0.33, respectively, compared to control conditions ([Firth, Torous, Nicholas, Carney, Pratap, et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R16); [Firth, Torous, Nicholas, Carney, Rosenbaum, et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R17)). Providing human support with a DMHI can enhance adherence and outcomes ([Baumeister, Reichler, Munzinger, & Lin, 2014](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R7); [Richards & Richardson, 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R50)).

Though using technology as an adjunct to psychotherapy can support mental health service delivery for those already receiving treatment, this paper focuses on delivering guided or unguided DMHIs in place of in-person specialty mental health services. This latter approach extends new services to those not receiving care and can increase access to practitioners by conserving time to deliver in-person services, thus enabling health care settings to deliver mental health services to more people in need. However, for practitioners to successfully use DMHIs in their practices, DMHIs have to be embedded in the workflows and tools they regularly use ([Greenhalgh et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R21)). By design, mental health services delivered as a DMHI differ from in-person psychotherapy, which means that DMHIs do not directly fit into traditional workflows. As a result, successful examples of integrating DMHIs into health care settings have not been consistently demonstrated: when DMHIs have moved from controlled trials to real-world settings, low uptake and engagement is common, and the tools are abandoned ([Greenhalgh et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R21); [Mohr, Lyon, Lattie, Reddy, & Schueller, 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R36)). The challenges of translating DMHIs from research to practice suggest more systematic approaches are needed to guide this process.

Implementation science is an emerging, rapidly growing field that has established theory, frameworks, methods, and strategies to enhance uptake and sustainability of innovations in real-world settings. Specific to DMHI implementation, there has been great progress in identifying facilitators and barriers (see [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#T1) and associated references in [Supplement 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#SD1)), establishing frameworks to predict and evaluate the success of patient-facing health care technologies (e.g., the Non-adoption, Abandonment, Scale-up, Spread, and Sustainability framework; [Greenhalgh et al., 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R21)), and defining implementation outcomes ([Hermes, Lyon, Schueller, & Glass, 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R25)). However, there remains a gap in knowing the methods and techniques (i.e., “implementation strategies;” [Powell et al., 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R43); [Powell et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R44); [Proctor, Powell, & McMillen, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R45)) for implementing a DMHI in health care settings. In general, adoption of an evidence-based service needs to be accompanied by an evidence-based approach to implementation ([Grol & Grimshaw, 1999](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R22)). Implementation strategies offer testable, replicable techniques to guide implementation, with growing evidence supporting their use ([Cochrane Collaboration, 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R11); [McMaster University, 2012](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R34); [Powell et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R42)). Yet to our knowledge, few implementation strategies specifically for implementing DMHIs in health care settings have been proposed ([Anton & Jones, 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R5); [LaMonica et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R29); [MasterMind, 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R32)) and fewer tested and disseminated. Because psychologists are often tasked with implementing or managing the implementation of new mental health services, for which DMHIs could be an attractive option, they need to be equipped with strategies that can guide DMHI implementation. Though some psychologists may heavily use DMHIs whereas others may be limited (e.g., depending on who they treat, practice characteristics), there is value in understanding the scope of work to support this process.

### Table 1.

Common barriers and facilitators for implementing digital mental health interventions in health care settings that have been documented in published reviews, and proposed implementation strategies.

**Common Barriers**
* * *
• Stigma associated with mental health and help seeking

• Preference for traditional delivery of face-to-face care

• Concerns with confidentiality, privacy, & data breeches

• Discomfort with or feeling incapable of using technology

• Complexity of the technology or intervention

• Mobile compatibility issues & interoperability with other systems

• Low digital literacy or awareness of DMHIs

• Limited research evidence for the DMHI

• Nonadherence & attrition• Organizational barriers (e.g., scheduling problems)

• No available technical support

•Limited staff resources and staff turn-over

• Lack of cultural and ethnic diversity

• Financial costs (e.g., reimbursement, start-up costs)

• Practitioners’ negative attitudes towards DMHIs

• Practitioners’ resistance to changes

• Practitioners’ perceived negative impact on consumer safety
* * *
**Common Facilitators**
* * *
• Advancements in technologies & quality of care

• Guidance from a health professional

• Enhancement of the therapeutic relationship/alliance

• Social support from online forums or groups, peer counseling

• User access & convenience

• Availability of online resources & text reminders

• Consumer-centric tools * features• Availability of developers

• Technical equipment and resources for implementation

• Liaisons to bridge cultural gaps

• Strong organizational leadership

• Standardized measures

• Maximum security, trust in and credibility of the DMHI

• Funding, lower costs, or cost analyses
* * *
**Proposed Implementation Strategies across Phases of Implementation**[*](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#TFN2)
* * *
**_Exploration Phase_**
• Conduct needs assessments(e.g., among practitioners, consumers)

• Align practitioners on DMHI adoption (e.g., consensus discussions)• Review DMHI evidence and content

• Aim to ensure equity in who can access the DMHI
**_Preparation & Implementation Phases_**
• Create a business associate agreement to restrict data usage

• Determine who is appropriate for the DMHI, and create guidelines

• Create and distribute educational materials about the DMHI

• Be transparent about DMHI data security, privacy, & use

• Assist with onboarding (e.g., educational materials,point-person)

• Create and disseminate practice guidelines for delivering the DMHI

• Offer training & ongoing supervision in using the DMHI

• Specify plans for monitoring & addressing safety concerns

• Change record systems (e.g., integrate DMHI with the health record; integrate communication portal with tools practitioners use)

• Appropriate sufficient funds (e.g., to license the DMHI, initiate a contract, workflow integration, programming, staff training)

• Build partnerships for priority setting & evaluation• Adopt DMHIs with demonstrated effectiveness

• Design the referral process & inform referring practitioners

• Have “champions” inform consumers about the DMHI

• Be transparent about DMHI requirements, promote autonomy

• Make technical assistance available

• Ensure practitioners are competent to deliver the DMHI

• Monitor practitioners’ fidelity to the DMHI protocol

• Make plans for safety monitoring transparent to consumers

• Conduct small tests of the new processes

• Track time & resources spent implementing the DMHI

• Create learning collaboratives to share resources & learnings
**_sustinment phase_**
• Optimize the technologies & implementation plans over time• Assess changing needs & preferences over time

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/table/T1/)

**Note:** DMHI = Digital mental health intervention. References associated with each barrier and facilitator are in [Supplement 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#SD1).

*
These four phases are from the Exploration, Preparation, Implementation, Sustainment (EPIS) Framework.

The aim of this paper is to enhance efforts to integrate DMHIs into health care settings that deliver mental health services by proposing a compilation of implementation strategies specific to DMHIs across different phases of implementation. The focus is on organization-level implementation of a DMHI [e.g., across a clinic(s), with a recurring budget line], rather than on DMHI adoption and delivery among individual practitioners, to account for organizational and contextual factors that impact sustained implementation of DMHIs in health care settings. Given that many discrete implementation strategies have been described for implementing innovations in general ([Powell et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R42); [Powell et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R44)), this paper presents a distilled compilation of these strategies and offers guidance in how they can be applied to DMHI implementation. Yet because the proposed strategies for DMHIs are primarily guided by theory and not empirical support for DMHI implementation, the paper closes with an agenda to propel future research.

Selecting Strategies for DMHI Implementation
--------------------------------------------

The Expert Recommendations for Implementing Change (ERIC) project established a compilation of 73 discrete implementation strategies through consensus among implementation and clinical practice experts ([Powell et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R44)). Using the ERIC compilation, implementation strategies for DMHI implementation were selected and operationalized to address DMHI-specific barriers in health care settings; this process occurred via narrative review followed by an iterative group consensus-building approach among the authors. In the sections that follow, the implementation strategies are described as they could be applied to DMHI implementation and for their relevance across four phases commonly distinguished in implementation science using the Exploration, Preparation, Implementation, Sustainment (EPIS) Framework ([Aarons, Hurlburt, & Horwitz, 2011](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R1); [Moullin, Dickson, Stadnick, Rabin, & Aarons, 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R37)). [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#T1) presents a summary of the proposed implementation strategies that are presented in this paper.

For this paper, a DMHI is the “evidence-based service” to which implementation strategies are suggested be applied. “Health care settings” are organizations with a mental health service in their practice. “Consumers” are the individuals who receive mental health services via the DMHI. “Practitioners” are the individuals who provide mental health services. Rather than referring only to psychologists, a broader term is used, as a range of practitioners provide mental health services in health care settings and can be trained to deliver a DMHI.

Strategies for DMHI Implementation in Health Care Settings
----------------------------------------------------------

### Exploration Phase Strategies

The exploration phase of implementation refers to the period when a setting considers whether to adopt an evidence-based service based on the needs of those in the setting ([Moullin et al., 2019](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R37)). Several factors can impact the decision to implement a DMHI and which one to adopt, such as alignment between the treatment needs of the setting and mental health problem(s) targeted by the DMHI, evidence supporting its efficacy, acceptability and usability by consumers and practitioners (e.g., likelihood the DMHI will meet consumers’ needs and integrate into practitioners’ workflow), and the cost to implement it in the setting (discussed in a later section).

Because many DMHIs are problem-specific, selecting a DMHI may require consensus among practitioners and administrators about which mental health problem(s) to target. The decision could be informed by assessing consumer-practitioner needs in that setting. For example, it may be helpful to adopt a DMHI that would address the most prevalent mental health problem facing the setting to reduce some burden for in-person services across practitioners. Alternatively, settings may benefit from adopting a DMHI that targets a less common problem but for which practitioner capacity to meet the demands of in-person services is limited. Once there is consensus on which problems to target, several DMHIs may be appropriate. Selection should be informed by reviewing the evidence and content for the DMHIs, as consumers and practitioners can have varying knowledge of these tools and their evidence (see [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#T1)).

The decision to adopt a DMHI also may be informed by ensuring the DMHI would be acceptable to consumers and practitioners in the setting. Prior to adoption, settings may engage with consumers to ensure they are willing and able to use this type of service. There are many reasons doing so may be indicated. For one, consumers may not be familiar with, interested in, or willing to use technology to receive mental health services. Because familiarity with a DMHI is associated with greater acceptance ([Vis et al., 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R58)), it is ideal for consumers to understand what a DMHI entails and how it differs from other mental health services. Concerns about privacy, data breaches, and the types and amount of digital data trails created by DMHI use ([Gandhi & Wang, 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R19); [Proudfoot et al., 2010](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R46); [Shilton, 2009](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R51); [Torous & Roberts, 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R56)) may make consumers reluctant to engage with a DMHI. Consumers also can be concerned about increased screen time that results from using a DMHI or the amount of time they spend using certain apps (e.g., [Baumer et al., 2013](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R8); [Ko et al., 2015](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R28)); this may be particularly salient to parents of youth given growing attention to limiting screen time ([Council on Communications and Media, 2016](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R13)). The mode of DMHI delivery (e.g., online, mobile) can affect access, in that not all consumers have a smartphone, regular access to the internet, adequate storage capacity on their smartphone for new apps, or a cellular plan (e.g., phone, text message, or data capacities) that would align with the DMHI. A DMHI may not be ideal for consumers who share devices with others.

If a DMHI includes support from a practitioner, the communication medium for the DMHI needs to be one that consumers can and would likely use. In 2018, only 35% and 29% of U.S. consumers communicated with a health care provider via email or the internet, or by text message, respectively ([Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology, 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R40)). Security limitations may also limit the acceptability of DMHI communication. For example, a DMHI may include text messaging, but in the U.S., sharing identifiable health information through text messaging and email are subject to ongoing debate related to policy guidelines around privacy, which are often not specific ([Drolet, 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R14); [Freundlich, Freundlich, & Drolet, 2017](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7709140/#R18)). As a result, health care settings may require that messages be delivered through hospital-approved messaging sites [e.g., MyChart, an online consumer-facing tool that can facilitate consumer-practitioner communication within the Epic electronic health record (EHR)]. However, consumers may not be interested in using such tools to receive mental health services.

Given these considerations, relevant implementation strategies that settings might pursue are to conduct a local needs assessment with consumers to determine whether the DMHI is relevant to their needs and preferences, such as by convening focus groups, conducting a survey, or through informal discussions, and to learn how a DMHI aligns with consumers’ existing perceptions of mental health services. Insights derived from these activities could inform the creation and distribution of educational materials about the DMHI and the decision to have “champions” available who can talk with consumers about the DMHI or provide testimonials.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Encourage audience participation by inviting questions to clarify concepts discussed about mental health and technology's role, fostering an interactive dialogue for deeper understanding.
Create a safe space for attendees to share personal stories related to mental health and technology use, helping to normalize discussions around these important issues.

enriched_points.point_title: Discuss strategies for healthy technology use
enriched_points.expanded_content: Introduce practical strategies such as setting screen time limits and creating tech-free zones, promoting healthier relationships with technology and minimizing its adverse effects on mental health.
Provide information on additional resources, such as apps and websites, that support mental health, reinforcing the importance of utilizing technology positively.
Express gratitude to attendees for their engagement and contributions, reinforcing the collective effort needed to address mental health challenges in the digital age.
